MARKET.head: Tequila auction record signals potential investment boom
MARKET.summ: With tequila hitting a new world auction record in recent weeks, and WineFi offering irs first premium tequila investment barrels, is the agave spirit set for a new boom? Arabella Mileham reports
takeaways: Clase Azul bottle sold for US$35,000 at Sotheby's in June, triple its estimate and a new tequila auction record.
Global tequila market valued at US$12.6bn, projected to reach US$21.6bn by 2033 with 9% growth.
Younger consumers driving demand for premium and super-premium tequila expressions.
WineFi launched fund of 50 premium tequila barrels maturing in Jalisco, citing constrained aged supply and 15-35% historic returns.
Extra Añejo classification requires minimum three years maturation; investment holding period recommended at up to ten years.

MARKET.head: Breaky Bottom, pioneering English vineyard, sells for first time since 1974
MARKET.summ: Breaky Bottom, one of the founding estates of the modern English wine movement, has been put up for sale for the first time since its establishment in 1974. The Sussex vineyard, created by late English wine pioneer Peter Hall, carries a guide price of £4 million
takeaways: Sussex estate founded by Peter Hall in 1974 listed at £4 million following his death in October 2025.
Breaky Bottom's sparkling wines served at King Charles III's coronation, Queen Elizabeth II's Platinum Jubilee, and London 2012 Olympics.
Estate produces approximately 10,000 bottles annually from six acres of vines, focusing on Seyval Blanc and Chardonnay-Pinot Noir blends.
Knight Frank expects international buyer interest, citing resilient demand for small-scale, terroir-driven producers over mass-market wine brands.
English wine production rose 39% year-on-year in 2025 to over 16.5 million bottles equivalent following favorable growing conditions.

MARKET.head: Babich Wines marks 110 years as New Zealand's rare independent family producer
MARKET.summ: From a Croatian immigrant filling barrels by hand to exports in more than 60 countries, Babich Wines' 110-year journey reflects the rise of New Zealand wine itself
takeaways: Croatian immigrant Josip Babich founded the winery in 1916, filling oak barrels by hand and selling locally during a 30-year struggle.
American servicemen stationed in New Zealand during World War II discovered Babich wines, providing crucial cash flow and launching export potential.
Second generation sons Peter and Joe modernised operations and built winemaking reputation; third generation David expanded internationally starting 2001.
A 1980s chance encounter with a German wine merchant sparked exports; by 1997 overseas sales exceeded domestic, now reaching 60+ countries.
Fourth generation began in 2025 when Peter Babich participated in his first vintage; company now operates 14 vineyards producing 750,000 cases annually.

MARKET.head: Tariffs force Franzia to shift from U.S. to Australian wine in China
MARKET.summ: Faced with nearly 80% import taxes, one of the world's best-selling boxed wine brands has abandoned U.S.-made wine in China, replacing it with Australian wine bottled locally
takeaways: Nearly 80% total tax burden on U.S. wine imports made Franzia's affordable pricing model unsustainable in China.
Franzia now sources Australian bulk wine and bottles it locally in China instead of importing finished U.S. boxes.
The Wine Group owns Australian winery assets that enabled the supply chain pivot to maintain market presence.
Local bottling allows faster packaging customization for Chinese e-commerce platforms and promotional cycles.
Brand recognition and price remain Franzia's competitive advantages despite products now labeled as China-bottled rather than U.S.-imported.

MARKET.head: Global wine awards boost Japanese producers' credibility, but sales impact remains unclear
MARKET.summ: Japanese wines have long been recognised by the likes of DWWA and IWSC, but for a country whose majority of wineries are mom-and-pop shops, do these accolades actually matter to producers looking to grow exports? Joyce Yip finds out
takeaways: Larger Japanese wineries like Château Mercian and Katsunuma Jyozo win international awards; family-operated producers rarely participate due to export costs.
Katsunuma Jyozo credits 2003-2004 Vinalies medals with securing distribution through Bernard Magrez Group in Europe.
Château Mercian saw significant impact from serving wine at 2016 G7 Summit rather than from awards alone.
Hong Kong importer says awards drive awareness but tastings and restaurant placements now matter more than competition medals.
Japanese wineries face production constraints from terrain and climate, limiting export capacity regardless of award recognition.

CULTURE.head: London Masterclass Explores Varied Styles of Modern Rosé
CULTURE.summ: Think you might be getting stuck in a rosé rut? Now you can find a pink wine that fits your present mood or preferred food at an evening masterclass in London on 10 September
takeaways: Masterclass on September 10 at The Chelsea Townhouse in London explores diverse rosé styles and food pairings.
Event hosted by Master of Wine Patrick Schmitt, limited to 28 guests, covers niche to mainstream pink wines.
Tasting designed for all knowledge levels, from casual drinkers to experienced wine enthusiasts.
Rosé producers can connect directly with consumers and gain media exposure through the drinks business and hotel.

CULTURE.head: Pol Roger releases 2019 Champagne from exceptional vintage harvest
CULTURE.summ: Champagne Pol Roger has unveiled its latest vintage expression from the exceptional 2019 harvest, and Patrick Schmitt MW gives his verdict
takeaways: 2019 vintage suffered frost, mildew, and hailstorms, reducing regional yields nearly 20% below average.
Ideal harvest conditions produced wines with rare harmony, freshness, and aromatic richness, per Pol Roger.
2019 achieved 10.7% average potential alcohol, exceeding the celebrated 2018 vintage at 10.2%.
First vintage under cellar master Damien Cambres features 63.5% Pinot Noir and 36.5% Chardonnay from premier and grand cru vineyards.
UK retailer prices 2019 at £365 per six-pack, 10% cheaper than 2018, with potential for 3-5 years cellaring.

CULTURE.head: Sam Neill, Two Paddocks founder, dies aged 78
CULTURE.summ: Sam Neill, the actor behind Jurassic Park's Dr Alan Grant and founder of Central Otago winery Two Paddocks, has died aged 78. While celebrated across the world for his film career, he spent more than three decades quietly building one of New Zealand's most respected Pinot Noir estates
takeaways: Actor planted first vineyard in 1993, built Two Paddocks into Central Otago's benchmark Pinot Noir producer over 30+ years.
Estate expanded across four sites in Gibbston, Alexandra, and Bannockburn—only Central Otago producer with vineyards in all three valleys.
Winery became certified organic from 2017 vintage, focused on modest production and estate-grown, site-specific Pinot Noir.
Neill rejected 'celebrity winemaker' label, championed Burgundian principles and screwcaps, argued New Zealand Pinot deserved recognition alongside Burgundy.
Death in Sydney was sudden and unexpected; he had announced cancer-free status earlier this year after 2023 diagnosis.

CULTURE.head: Nyetimber wins third Great Britain top winery title; Coates & Seely surges to podium
CULTURE.summ: Last year Nyetimber topped the charts in this comprehensive blind tasting of English wines carried out by Australian publication The Real Review. But has the Sussex producer managed to hold onto its crown in 2026, or has it been eclipsed by another contender? Anthony Rose reports
takeaways: Nyetimber retained its crown as top winery in The Real Review's blind tasting, winning the title three times and consecutively.
Coates & Seely vaulted from 12th to 3rd place, one of the most dramatic single-year rises in the competition's history.
Hampshire emerged as the most dynamic region, with multiple producers improving standings and challenging Kent and Sussex's traditional dominance.
Ten new wineries entered the ranked list of 39 producers, with Domaine Hugo debuting at 18th place.
English sparkling wine producers showed broad consistency across regions, with still Chardonnays from multiple regions gaining recognition.

CULTURE.head: Berkeley Wine Festival to Focus on Unconventional Producers
CULTURE.summ: The two-day event will showcase pours and hot discussion topics from producers who are supported and promoted beyond the festival July 13, 2026 (Berkeley, CA) , Unfiltered: A Celebration of
takeaways: Two-day event scheduled for July 13, 2026 in Berkeley, California.
Festival will feature pours from producers outside mainstream wine industry.
Organizers plan to host discussions on forward-thinking wine topics.
Participating producers receive promotion and support beyond the festival itself.

SCIENCE.head: Grape sunburn becomes widespread concern as heat waves intensify globally
SCIENCE.summ: As climate change makes heat waves more prevalent, more producers have to combat sunburn in grapes, and the flabby, flat, rubbery notes they can bring to wine
takeaways: Sunburn occurs when berry surface temperatures exceed 104-109°F under high light, causing chlorophyll and aromatic compounds to degrade within minutes.
Affected wines display cooked fruit, rubber, asphalt, and creosote aromas alongside flabby acid and premature aging characteristics.
Mid-to-late summer defoliation now risks sunburn damage; early-season leaf removal allows berries to build protective compounds before heat stress.
Producers increasingly use kaolin clay particle film and canopy management to keep fruit shaded rather than traditionally exposing grapes to direct sun.
Climate change is pushing sunburn damage into traditionally cool regions, making prevention a critical practice beyond traditional hot-climate viticulture areas.

SCIENCE.head: French heatwave slashes grape yields across Champagne, Bordeaux, Burgundy
SCIENCE.summ: Weeks of drought are reducing yields and raising quality concerns, though weak global demand means shortages are far from certain
takeaways: Champagne expects grape production to fall roughly 10% year-over-year; Bordeaux and Burgundy anticipate declines of 20% or more.
Extreme heat damages vine leaves, reduces berry size, and risks raising alcohol levels while lowering acidity in finished wines.
Many regions plan to harvest a month earlier than historical norms, with Champagne potentially picking around August 15.
Premium Burgundy wines face tighter supplies due to structural undersupply; entry-level Bordeaux unlikely to create shortages given existing excess inventory.
Some Bordeaux estates irrigate vines despite AOC restrictions; irrigation now authorized in Pauillac to mitigate heat stress.

SCIENCE.head: Heat-Loving Mourvèdre Struggles as Southern France Temperatures Rise
SCIENCE.summ: In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
takeaways: Mourvèdre thrives in hot, dry climates but needs moderation—traditionally needing 'its head in the sun and its feet in the sea.'.
Producers in Bandol are experimenting with canopy management techniques like high gobelet training to shade grapes from extreme heat and prevent sunburn.
Drought poses a critical challenge; some viticulturists argue irrigation is now essential for survival, while dry-farming advocates worry it will compromise terroir typicity.
Jumilla's vineyard area has halved in 20 years as lower-altitude, hotter sites are abandoned; remaining quality parcels sit at higher elevations with better temperature variation.
Bandol's cooler sites traditionally used for rosé production are being reconsidered for red wine as climate patterns shift, potentially reducing the region's 75% rosé output.

SCIENCE.head: Organic Sauvignon Blanc proves challenging but rewarding to cultivate
SCIENCE.summ: From fruity and tropical to stony and herbal, there's a Sauvignon style for everyone, if you know where to look. These organic examples are perfect for summer
takeaways: Sauvignon Blanc's sensitivity to powdery and downy mildew makes organic cultivation difficult, requiring frequent treatments.
Copper-based fungicides permitted in organic farming can damage Sauvignon Blanc's flowers, creating a management dilemma.
Biodynamic farming builds living soil and helps overcome climate risks, according to practitioners in France and Austria.
Years of organic farming can increase vineyard resilience to disease, as demonstrated by producers in southern France.
Sauvignon Blanc flavor profiles range from tropical and exotic to citrusy, grassy, mineral, depending on climate and production methods.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Highlights: Sandridge Barton East Field Pinot Noir Early 2025 Devon, England
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Another cracking lighter-style red from the UK! Sandridge Barton East Field Pinot Noir Early 2025 Devon, England11.2% alcohol. 100% Pinot
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jamie Goode
takeaways: Sandridge Barton East Field Pinot Noir Early 2025 from Devon, England: 11.2% alcohol, 100% Pinot Noir Precoce.
Production: 30% whole bunch, vatted 8 days, aged 8 months in stainless steel, no sulfites added.
Tasting profile: crunchy, vivid raspberry and cherry fruit, primary, vibrant, sweet texture. Scored 93/100.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Portland, Maine: Come Hungry
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hey, just a small catch-up after a few sweltering days at Pinewoods Dance Camp, where there was at least one day it was just too damned hot to leave Long Pond and balance and swing
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alice Feiring
takeaways: Alice Feiring visited Portland, Maine and found cabbage dishes to be consistently excellent.
Feiring recommends Portland, Maine as a destination worth visiting for food.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Mountains and Magliocco
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
NEWSLETTERS.author: Simon J. Woolf
takeaways: Calabria has nine scattered DOCs; only Cirò has achieved fame, known for red Gaglioppo with punchy tannins and tart cherry fruit.
Magliocco comes in two variants: Magliocco Dolce (more tannic, eastern Calabria) and Magliocco Canino (retains acidity, extremely late ripening, potentially related to Sangiovese).
Magliocco Canino's 2017 rosé was harvested in early December; October harvests remain normal even today, showing its late-ripening character.
Despite proximity to the Tyrrhenian coast, Cirò wines dominate local wine lists; Librandi controls most by-the-glass options in Tropea, 200km and three hours' drive away.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Sysco-ification of Wine
NEWSLETTERS.summ: How wine culture's lazy "drink what you like" ethos has failed, and why it's time to break out the pitchforks
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jason Wilson
takeaways: Gallo controlled 33% of U.S. wine market in 2023 and has since acquired Rombauer, Hahn Family Wines, Massican, Whiny Baby, and Bev canned wine.
Top 11 wine suppliers control 71% of U.S. wine, according to 2024 American Association of Wine Economists data.
Independent wine distributors dropped from roughly 3,000 in the 1990s to just over 1,000 today.
Three dominant distributors—Breakthru, RNDC, and Southern Glazer's—controlled 53% of wine and spirits distribution in America as of 2024.
Restaurant wine lists nationwide are dominated by mass-market brands from a handful of corporate wineries, creating an illusion of choice.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Summer 2026 Natural Wine Salons
NEWSLETTERS.summ: A calendar of upcoming natural wine salons for June, July, and August 2026. Plus: interviews with visionary Alsace vignerons Bruno Schueller and Jean-Marc Dreyer
NEWSLETTERS.author: Aaron Ayscough
takeaways: Sous Les Pavés, La Vigne in Paris (June 6-8) will be its 25th and final edition, featuring 60 natural vignerons with a strong Alsace contingent.
Nach-Barn Wine Fest, a new salon organized by Jan Culik, launches August 1st in Znojmo to foster cross-cultural exchange between Moravian and Austrian natural winemakers.
Over 40 natural wine salons are scheduled across France, Italy, Germany, UK, and Czech Republic between May 30 and August 2026.
